title: Application of objective optical quality analysis system in guiding early posterior capsule opacification laser treatment: An observational study.
abstract: In the present study, we aimed to demonstrate the objective guiding role of objective scatter index (OSI) in patients with early stage posterior capsule opacification (PCO), especially in patients with multifocal intraocular lens (IOL) implantation. A prospective randomized case analysis was conducted on elderly patients experiencing PCO, exhibiting best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) below 0.2 LogMAR. Exclusion criteria comprised patients with diabetes, glaucoma, high myopia, trauma, endothelial dystrophy, maculopathy or prior eye surgery affecting visual acuity. The main observational indicators were patient gender, age, post-cataract surgery duration, BCVA (LogMAR), and OSI values. For patients undergoing laser posterior capsulotomy, meticulous documentation of pre-laser OSI values and post-laser OSI values was undertaken. Subjective visual quality was then compared using the Chinese version visual function index-14 (VF-14-CN). The study comprised 65 eyes from 56 patients. PCO developed, on average, 2.06 ± 0.94 years (ranging from 0.5 to 4 years) after the initial surgery, with the OSI consistently exceeding 3.0. Among these cases, only 40 eyes underwent laser posterior capsulotomy using Nd:YAG laser, including 26 eyes implanted with monofocal IOLs and 14 eyes implanted with multifocal IOLs. Following laser posterior capsulotomy for early-stage secondary cataracts, there was a significant decrease in OSI values compared to the presurgery state, coupled with a notable increase in VF-14-CN scores. Among the 14 cases with multifocal IOL implantation, pre-laser VF-14-CN scores were lower compared to those with monofocal IOLs. Post-laser posterior capsulotomy, patients reported a marked improvement in self-perceived symptoms. The OQAS functioned as an objective assessment metric for the laser treatment of early stage PCO. In cases involving multifocal IOL implantation, the visual disturbance caused by PCO was more pronounced, underscoring the significance of OSI as a valuable guide for laser treatment in these patients.
full_text:
Application of objective optical quality analysis system in guiding early posterior capsule opacification laser treatment: An observational study

In the present study, we aimed to demonstrate the objective guiding role of objective scatter index (OSI) in patients with early stage posterior capsule opacification (PCO), especially in patients with multifocal intraocular lens (IOL) implantation. A prospective randomized case analysis was conducted on elderly patients experiencing PCO, exhibiting best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) below 0.2 LogMAR. Exclusion criteria comprised patients with diabetes, glaucoma, high myopia, trauma, endothelial dystrophy, maculopathy or prior eye surgery affecting visual acuity. The main observational indicators were patient gender, age, post-cataract surgery duration, BCVA (LogMAR), and OSI values. For patients undergoing laser posterior capsulotomy, meticulous documentation of pre-laser OSI values and post-laser OSI values was undertaken. Subjective visual quality was then compared using the Chinese version visual function index-14 (VF-14-CN). The study comprised 65 eyes from 56 patients. PCO developed, on average, 2.06 ± 0.94 years (ranging from 0.5 to 4 years) after the initial surgery, with the OSI consistently exceeding 3.0. Among these cases, only 40 eyes underwent laser posterior capsulotomy using Nd:YAG laser, including 26 eyes implanted with monofocal IOLs and 14 eyes implanted with multifocal IOLs. Following laser posterior capsulotomy for early-stage secondary cataracts, there was a significant decrease in OSI values compared to the presurgery state, coupled with a notable increase in VF-14-CN scores. Among the 14 cases with multifocal IOL implantation, pre-laser VF-14-CN scores were lower compared to those with monofocal IOLs. Post-laser posterior capsulotomy, patients reported a marked improvement in self-perceived symptoms. The OQAS functioned as an objective assessment metric for the laser treatment of early stage PCO. In cases involving multifocal IOL implantation, the visual disturbance caused by PCO was more pronounced, underscoring the significance of OSI as a valuable guide for laser treatment in these patients.

1. Introduction

With the advancements in cataract phacoemulsification surgery and the utilization of various functional intraocular lenses (IOLs), cataract surgery has transitioned from the age of sight restoration to the era of refractive surgery. Consequently, postoperative expectations regarding visual quality have steadily increased. However, despite these improvements, reports on posterior capsule opacification (PCO), the most common complication impacting visual function after cataract surgery, remain abundant, particularly in the context of various multifocal IOL implantation procedures.

The combined utilization of slit-lamp examination and objective visual acuity assessment stands as a widely employed method in clinical settings for evaluating PCO. In instances where pronounced opacification of the posterior capsule is accompanied by significant visual impairment, the unequivocal intervention of choice is posterior capsulotomy. However, with the evolution of refractive cataract surgery and the increasing emphasis on visual quality, clinicians frequently encounter a subset of patients in their practice. These individuals may not manifest overt visual deterioration but seek consultation due to the gradual onset of visual blurring following cataract surgery. Slit-lamp examination reveals varying degrees of PCO in these cases. Determining the optimal timing for laser intervention in these early stage PCO remains a formidable challenge confronting ophthalmologists in their clinical practice.

The optical quality analysis system (OQAS) introduces a novel approach through a dual-channel system for the objective and comprehensive evaluation of visual quality. This system, by providing an objective assessment of cataract staging and grading, plays a pivotal role in guiding clinical decision-making concerning optimal treatment timing and finds extensive applications in clinical settings. Our preliminary research also suggests its valuable guidance in addressing PCO. Consequently, in the objective assessment of early-stage PCO, can the optical scatter index (OSI) values for laser treatment be referenced from the OSI values utilized in cataract surgery? Furthermore, does the guiding role of OQAS equally apply to multifocal IOLs? With this notion in mind, we initiated the following clinical study.

2. Materials and methods

2.1. Study population

Data were collected from elderly patients attending outpatient services at Jinan Second People’s Hospital between May 2022 and November 2023. Inclusion criteria involved patients who had undergone cataract surgery and subsequently developed secondary cataracts, although exhibiting a best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) below 0.2 LogMAR. Patients with diabetes, glaucoma, ocular surface diseases, high myopia, trauma, endothelial dystrophy, maculopathy, or a history of previous eye surgery affecting visual acuity were excluded. The main observational parameters included patient gender, age, post-cataract surgery duration, BCVA, and the OSI derived from the OQAS. Patients undergoing laser posterior capsulotomy underwent meticulous documentation of pre-laser and post-laser OSI values. Additionally, a subjective assessment of visual function was conducted using the Chinese version visual function index-14 (VF-14-CN).

Ethical approval for this study was granted by the Ethics Committee of Jinan Second People’s Hospital, and all participating patients provided informed consent.

2.2. Examination methods

2.2.1. Routine examinations

Standard assessments included uncorrected visual acuity using a 5-meter visual acuity chart, BCVA examination conducted through auto refract-keratometer and phoroptor, intraocular pressure measurement, endothelial cell count, B-scan ultrasonography, slit-lamp examination, and dilated fundus examination. Given that most cases presented with good BCVA despite prominent subjective symptoms, optical coherence tomography (OCT) was performed to exclude early macular lesions.

2.2.2. OQAS examination

OQAS examinations were conducted using a dual-channel device (OQAS II Optical Quality Analysis System, OQAS TM II, Visiometrics, Spain) to measure the OSI. The examination was performed without dilation, with a pupil diameter set at 4 mm. Patients’ refractive errors were corrected internally within the range of −8D to + 6D, and astigmatism was corrected by placing appropriate cylindrical lenses in front of the eye. Due to the impact of tear film quality on light scattering, all examinations were carried out after the patient blinked once.

2.2.3. VF-14-CN visual function index examination

The VF-14 serves as a widely employed tool for assessing visual function and quality of life among cataract patients. In this investigation, the Chinese version of VF-14 was utilized to gauge patients’ subjective impairment in visual function. Under the guidance of the same ophthalmologist, patients completed the questionnaire, affirming whether their daily activities were impacted by visual function impairment, accounting for correction with glasses. Severity levels were stratified into 5 categories (unable to perform, severe difficulty, moderate difficulty, mild difficulty, and no difficulty) and scored on a scale from 0 to 4, respectively. A higher score denoted a more favorable level of visual function.

2.2.4. Posterior capsulotomy through Nd:YAG laser

Before posterior capsulotomy, all pupils were dilated to 6 mm using tropicamide eye drops (Tropicamide Phenylephrine Eye Drops, Japan). All laser treatments were performed using an Nd:YAG laser (VISULAS YAG III, Zeiss Germany) with a capsulotomy lens. Research suggests that excessive posterior capsulotomy following multifocal IOL implantation can induce a certain degree of hyperopic shift, thereby affecting the overall visual acuity of multifocal IOLs. To mitigate this risk, in our study, we utilized lower energy levels during laser treatment and confined the posterior capsulotomy within a central 4.5-mm diameter range.

2.3. Statistical methods

Statistical analyses were conducted using SPSS 25.0 software. Paired-sample t tests were employed to evaluate the pre-laser and post-laser OSI values, as well as the comparisons derived from the VF-14 questionnaire. Mann–Whitney U tests were utilized for other comparative analyses. Pearson correlation analysis was applied to assess correlations. A significance level of P < .05 was deemed statistically significant for all reported results.

3. Results

As depicted in Table 1, the study encompassed a total of 65 eyes from 56 patients, and the onset of PCO occurred at an average of 2.06 ± 0.94 years after cataract surgery, with a range from 0.5 to 4 years. Within this cohort, 40 eyes underwent laser posterior capsulotomy. Among these, 26 eyes received monofocal IOL implantation, and 14 eyes underwent multifocal IOL implantation.

Patient demographic information.

Time form cataract surgery to YAG treatment.

3.1. BCVA and OSI in patients with early stage PCO

All patients with PCO included in the study exhibited a BCVA equal to or worsen than 0.2 LogMAR. The average OSI value for the 65 eyes in the study was 3.96 ± 0.78, with a range from 3.0 to 5.9. Figure 1 represented by the box plot, visually demonstrates a gradual decrease in BCVA as OSI values increased. However, the relationship between BCVA and OSI values was not found to be statistically significant (R = 0.144, P > .05). It was noteworthy that all 65 eyes in the study exhibited OSI values above 3.0.

Box plot of OSI and BCVA in eyes with early PCO. It showed that all 65 eyes in the study had OSI values above 3.0, and BCVA gradually decreased as OSI values increased, but the relationship between BCVA and OSI values was not statistically significant. BCVA = best-corrected visual acuity, OSI = optical scatter index, PCO = posterior capsule opacification.

3.2. Changes in BCVA and OSI before and after laser posterior capsulotomy

As outlined in Table 2, Figures 2, and 3, 40 eyes underwent laser posterior capsulotomy. The average BCVA before laser treatment was 0.12 ± 0.07 (ranging from 0 to 0.2) LogMAR, and the mean OSI value was 4.0 ± 0.78 (ranging from 3.0 to 5.9). At the 1-month follow-up after the procedure, the average BCVA improved to 0.05 ± 0.06 (ranging from 0 to 0.2) LogMAR, and the mean OSI value reduced to 0.82 ± 0.19 (ranging from 0.5 to 1.3). Comparison of pre- and post-laser values revealed a significant enhancement in BCVA (t = 5.597, P = .000) and a substantial decrease in OSI values (t = 23.305, P = .000), with both differences demonstrating statistical significance.

Comparative analysis of parameters following laser capsulotomy procedure.

3.3. OSI changes before and after laser posterior capsulotomy in patients with multifocal IOLs

As illustrated in Table 2 and Figure 3, among the study participants, 14 eyes were associated with patients who had undergone multifocal IOL implantation. Prior to laser posterior capsulotomy, the average LogMAR visual acuity was 0.15 ± 0.05 (ranging from 0.05 to 0.2), and the OSI was 3.5 ± 0.32 (ranging from 3.1 to 4.2). Following the procedure, the mean LogMAR visual acuity improved to 0.03 ± 0.04 (ranging from 0.0 to 0.1), and the OSI reduced to 0.8 ± 0.14 (ranging from 0.6 to 1.0). Statistically significant differences were observed in LogMAR visual acuity before and after laser posterior capsulotomy (t = 8.94, P = .00), as well as in OSI values (t = 32.68, P = .00).

In the comparison between patients with multifocal IOLs and those with monofocal IOLs, as shown in Table 1, it was noted that the time from cataract surgery to the onset of secondary cataracts was shorter in the multifocal group (1.15 ± 0.45 years) compared to the monofocal group (2.34 ± 0.92 years), and this difference was statistically significant (Z = −3.86, P = .00).

3.4. VF-14-CN results

As outlined in Table 2, the VF-14-CN index analysis revealed that among the 40 eyes undergoing laser posterior capsulotomy, the preoperative VF-14 index stood at 55.31 ± 8.43 (ranging from 35.71 to 69.64), exhibiting a certain correlation with preoperative BCVA (r = −0.323, P = .04). As illustrated in Figure 4, post-laser treatment, the VF index markedly increased to 92.15 ± 2.22 (ranging from 85.71 to 96.64), displaying a significant difference from the preoperative values (t = −26.137, P = .00), indicating a substantial enhancement in patient satisfaction. This improvement in satisfaction was also reflected in both eyes, whether implanted with monofocal IOLs or multifocal IOLs. Additionally, as illustrated in Table 2, the comparison of PCO after monofocal and multifocal IOL implantation revealed statistical significance in the VF-14 index before laser treatment (Z = −4.48, P = .00). Patients with multifocal IOL implantation exhibited lower subjective satisfaction. However, the difference in VF-14 index after laser posterior capsulotomy was not significant (Z = −0.46, P = .65).

The change in VF-14 before and after laser capsulotomy procedure. The VF-14 scores improved after laser capsulotomy, and the change was statistically significant (⁕ P < .05). VF-14-CN = Chinese version visual function index-14.

4. Discussion

In recent years, objective OQAS based on dual-channel systems have progressively found application in the objective assessment of cataracts. Among these systems, the OSI emerges as a quantitative analytical tool for evaluating the severity of cataracts, characterized by high accuracy and operational simplicity, showcasing promising clinical applicability. Artal et al and Pan et al, in their objective assessments of cataracts utilizing OQAS, propose that an OSI value equal to or exceeding 3.0 can serve as a reference for determining the optimal timing of cataract surgery. In our study, 65 eyes with early stage PCO underwent OSI examinations. We observed that patients reporting subjective visual functional decline consistently exhibited OSI values equal to or exceeding 3.0.

Among the 65 eyes, 25 eyes were not further analyzed due to the lack of laser capsulotomy. An examination of the remaining 40 eyes that underwent laser treatment revealed a significant improvement in visual function and a notable decrease in OSI values. Therefore, in the objective assessment of PCO, we proposed that the OSI value used to determine the timing of laser treatment could be similarly referenced to the OSI value for cataract surgery. However, with the escalating demands for visual quality, Li et al have found that even early-stage cataract patients with OSI values below 3.0 experience substantial subjective visual functional improvement following cataract surgery. This suggests that perhaps early stage PCO patients with OSI values below 3.0 may also benefit from laser posterior capsulotomy, though this requires further validation through additional clinical research.

The presence of PCO significantly affects patients’ visual quality, as evidenced by an elevation in the scatter index within dual-channel systems. However, individual variations exist among patients, as depicted in our box plot (Fig. 1). Similar visual acuity levels may correspond to different OSI values, and conversely, identical OSI values may correspond to varying BCVAs. We hypothesized that this phenomenon arose from the uneven distribution of opacification in the posterior capsule of PCO. Patients with a centrally transparent gap might exhibit better BCVA but OSI measured the scattering effect within a 4-mm pupil diameter range, allowing early detection of visual function decline. This might elucidate why objective OQAS could identify PCO earlier and offer objective guidance on the timing of laser treatment.

Lu et al, in their research, have similarly found that different types of PCOs exhibit distinct morphologies and severities, leading to varying degrees of light scattering and subsequent impact on visual function. They conduct studies and validation using OSI values and contrast sensitivity visual acuity within the objective OQAS. While we did not differentiate between types PCO due to sample size considerations, our speculations and conclusions aligned with their findings.

The VF-14 is a subjective measurement tool originally developed for assessing the quality of life in cataract patients in developed countries. To better align with the domestic context, we employed an adapted Chinese version of the visual function index (VF-14-CN), incorporating modifications such as daytime and nighttime driving considerations based on specific patient situations, including cars, electric bikes, or bicycles. Our results demonstrated a gradual decline in VF-14 scores corresponding to the decrease in patients’ BCVA, consistent with prior research. Despite all our patients being in the early stages of PCO, their VF-14 scores exhibited a decline. Following laser posterior capsulotomy, as patients’ subjective satisfaction increased, there was a corresponding significant rise in VF-14 scores. This observed change aligned with alterations in OSI, suggesting that OSI served as a reliable objective evaluation method for visual quality closely correlated with patients’ subjective experiences. This underscored the guiding significance of OSI in laser treatment decisions. With the continuous progress of refractive cataract surgery, the widespread adoption of multifocal IOLs in clinical practice has become more prominent. While the incidence of PCO after the implantation of multifocal IOLs varies based on different materials and designs, it consistently remains higher compared to monofocal IOLs, leading to a greater proportion of laser posterior capsulotomy. This heightened incidence may be attributed to distinct design principles, resulting in lower contrast sensitivity with multifocal IOLs in contrast to monofocal IOLs. Even subtle opacities or folds in the posterior capsule can further diminish contrast sensitivity, increase light scattering, and exacerbate glare and other ocular discomfort. These factors collectively magnify the impact of PCO on the visual quality of patients after multifocal IOL implantation.

In our present study, in comparison to monofocal IOLs, patients with PCO following multifocal IOL implantation exhibited lower VF-14-CN scores, and the time elapsed since cataract surgery was shorter. This finding substantiated the notion that the influence on visual quality was more pronounced in patients with PCO after multifocal IOL implantation than those with monofocal IOLs.

Slit-lamp examinations 1-month post-laser treatment revealed excellent centration for all multifocal IOLs, and subjective questionnaire surveys, as well as OSI measurements, demonstrated statistically significant improvements compared to preoperative values. These findings indicated that laser treatment was an effective intervention for managing PCO following multifocal IOL implantation. Moreover, existing research suggests that early laser intervention for PCO after multifocal IOL implantation holds substantial significance in improving patients’ visual quality and preventing severe complications.

Certainly, this study has certain limitations. The sample size was relatively small, particularly with only 14 eyes included in the study for patients with multifocal IOL implantation. Therefore, we did not differentiate between types of PCO. Additionally, as this was a randomized outpatient study, we did not distinguish between different types of multifocal IOLs. Given the diverse range of multifocal IOLs available with the advancement of refractive surgery, differentiation might offer more meaningful insights and would be a direction for further research.

In conclusion, based on this study, we posited that the OSI from the dual-channel OQAS could quantitatively evaluate early stage PCO. An OSI value equal to or >3 served as an objective criterion for selecting laser treatment. Particularly in cases of early stage PCO following multifocal IOL implantation, where the impact on visual function was more pronounced, the objective assessment role of OSI surpassed mere visual acuity checks. Moreover, it provided effective guidance for laser treatment.
